content.assessment: This proposal targets the geographic/settlement channel directly, which the demographic evidence identifies as a distinct and significant driver of stratification (village-to-vocational funneling, choice-driven commuting effects) operating independently of admissions-exam design. It is well-matched to the geography-sensitive nature of Hungary's segregation pattern, but its durability is constrained by the same centralization (KLIK) that limits local capacity to respond to demographic contraction.
opportunities: Ongoing school consolidation (270 endangered schools) creates a natural policy window to redraw zoning and catchment boundaries in ways that counteract settlement-based segregation as networks are restructured anyway
strengths: Directly engages the settlement-level concentration pattern (48% small towns, 3% villages) identified as a structural, geography-specific driver of segregation distinct from admissions timing
Targets choice/commuting mechanisms shown to correlate with inter-school inequality, addressing a channel that would persist even under comprehensive de-tracking
threats: Legal exemption precedent (2015 Curia ruling) could be invoked to resist zoning reforms in a shrinking-cohort environment where maintainers have incentive to preserve selective enrollment pools
Limited local adaptation capacity under centralization may leave rural areas unable to respond effectively as their cohorts continue to contract disproportionately
weaknesses: Historical precedent shows administrative desegregation rules from the 2000s achieved only temporary reductions before stagnating, raising doubts about durability under continued demographic pressure
Centralization under KLIK curtails the municipal planning autonomy needed to execute zoning-level responses to local cohort decline
